genes.gene_name: Ruminococcus gnavus pro-inflammatory polysaccharide biosynthetic genes (23-gene set)

Source_Species.reason: The paper explicitly searched for 23 R. gnavus genes related to biosynthesis of a pro-inflammatory polysaccharide.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Ruminococcus gnavus is explicitly named throughout the Results.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: The Results state that allergy-associated R. gnavus strains had higher prevalence of these genes.

Applied_Species.reason: The metagenomes came from human children.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Human children correspond to Homo sapiens.

Omics_Data.reason: The Methods state shotgun metagenomics, SRA PRJNA706116, assembly, and BlastX alignment against R. gnavus genes coding for an inflammatory polysaccharide.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: The paper describes these as genes related to biosynthesis/production of a pro-inflammatory polysaccharide, i.e., a biosynthetic cluster/signature.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: Discussion identifies the polysaccharide as a glucorhamnan from prior work and links the searched genes to its production.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: The paper repeatedly calls it a pro-inflammatory polysaccharide.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The paper explicitly describes it as pro-inflammatory and as a potential mechanism leading to inflammation.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: The paper does not explicitly state 'bacterial surface / gut lumen' for this polysaccharide in this study.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Results show a significantly higher number of hits to these genes in FA and RA compared with healthy children, indicating greater prevalence in allergy-associated strains.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Supported by targeted mapping to the 23 genes, strain-level pangenome analysis, and classification using presence/absence of the 23 genes.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results explicitly state allergic children had a significantly higher number of hits against these genes.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: The paper analyzes these genes by food/respiratory allergy status and also notes discrimination for single vs multiple allergen sensitization.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: The paper links allergy-associated R. gnavus strains both to inflammation-related genes and to depletion of fiber-degradation genes.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The paper does not discuss breeding/engineering applications or explicitly propose strain-selective intervention targeting this gene set.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All summary points are consistent with Results: more biosynthetic genes in allergy-associated strains, good healthy-vs-allergic classification using the 23-gene matrix, and strain-dependent inflammatory functions.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The paper indeed treats them as a 23-gene biosynthetic signature rather than detailing each individual gene name.

corrections.old_value: Potential microbiome diagnostic marker and target for strain-selective microbiome intervention
corrections.reason: The paper does not discuss breeding/engineering applications or explicitly propose strain-selective intervention targeting this gene set.
